title: Max-Heap Structure & Array Mapping
examTip: A Max-Heap is a complete binary tree where parent >= children. Because it is a complete binary tree, it is space-efficiently represented using a 1-based array. For index i: left child = 2i, right child = 2i+1, parent = floor(i/2). Creation (heapify) takes O(n) time, insertion takes O(log n) via bubble-up, and deletion of max takes O(log n) via bubble-down.
theory:
A Max-Heap is a specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property: if P is a parent node of C, then the key (the value) of P is greater than or equal to the key of C. This means that the largest element in the heap is always stored at the root, facilitating constant-time O(1) access to the maximum element.

In addition to the heap property, a heap is a complete binary tree. A binary tree is complete if all levels are completely filled except possibly the lowest level, which is filled from left to right. This structural constraint ensures that a heap of n nodes always has a height of floor(log2(n)), providing a guarantee of O(log n) time complexity for insertions and deletions.

Array Representation:
Because of the complete binary tree structure, heaps can be stored efficiently in an array without any pointer overhead. Standard implementations (such as in the Horowitz and Sahni textbook) use 1-based indexing:
1. The root element is stored at index 1 (index 0 is left empty or unused).
2. For any node at index i:
   - Its left child is located at index 2*i.
   - Its right child is located at index 2*i + 1.
   - Its parent is located at index floor(i/2).

Basic Operations:
- Insertion (O(log n)): Insert the new element at the end of the array (maintaining the complete tree structure). Then, compare the new element with its parent. If it is greater than the parent, swap them. Repeat this 'bubble-up' (heapify-up) process until the heap property is restored.
- Deletion of Max (O(log n)): Remove the root element (index 1), which contains the maximum value. Replace it with the last element of the array. Then, compare this new root with its children. If it is smaller than either child, swap it with the larger child. Repeat this 'bubble-down' (heapify-down) process recursively down the tree until the heap property is restored.
keyPoints: Max-Heap Property: The key of each node is greater than or equal to the keys of its children (Key(parent) >= Key(child)).
Complete Binary Tree constraint: All levels are fully filled, except the last which is filled from left to right.
Array mapping (1-based): Left child = 2i, Right child = 2i + 1, Parent = floor(i/2). Index 0 is left unused.
Accessing the maximum element is O(1) as it is always at the root (index 1).
Insert and Delete operations run in O(log n) time, keeping the tree balanced.
Heapify operation: Builds a heap from an unordered array in O(n) time by heapifying down from index floor(n/2) to 1.
useCases: Priority Queues: Heaps provide an efficient implementation of priority queues, supporting quick insertion and extraction of maximum/minimum elements.
Heapsort Algorithm: An in-place comparison-based sorting algorithm with a guaranteed O(n log n) time complexity in the worst, average, and best cases.
Graph Algorithms: Used to implement Dijkstra's shortest path and Prim's minimum spanning tree algorithms efficiently using min-heaps.

blocks.label: Max-Heap Structure & Array Mapping (Ellis Horowitz Convention)
Max-Heap Tree Property
sublabels: • Complete Binary Tree: Levels filled left-to-right
• Value of Parent >= Values of its Children
• Root node (i = 1) always holds the maximum key (90)

blocks.label: 1-Based Array Indexing Formulas
sublabels: • Left Child of index i = 2 * i
• Right Child of index i = 2 * i + 1
